description: Main psychological definitions and formulas that are not part of the units, physics, math or chemistry base data.

words.name: psychology
words.description: study of mental functions and behaviours

words.name: behaviorism
words.description: approach to psychology

words.name: neuropsychology
words.description: study of the brain related to specific psychological processes and behaviors

words.name: psychometrics
words.description: study of the theory and technique of psychological measurement

words.name: psychophysics
words.description: scientific study of perceptual systems

words.name: cognition
words.description: mental action or process of acquiring knowledge and understanding through thought, experience, and the senses

words.name: memory
words.description: mental faculties and processes involved in storing and retrieving information

words.name: perception
words.description: organization, identification, and interpretation of sensory information in order to represent and understand the environment in the brain

words.name: attention
words.description: behavioral and cognitive process of selectively concentrating on a discrete aspect of information, whether deemed subjective or objective, while ignoring other perceivable information

words.name: reasoning
words.description: the capacity of consciously making sense of things, applying logic, and adapting or justifying practices, institutions, and beliefs based on new or existing information

words.name: thought
words.description: mental activity involving an individual's subjective consciousness

words.name: consciousness
words.description: knowledge that a being has of its external and internal existence and its environment

words.name: creativity
words.description: cognitive process whereby something new and somehow valuable is formed

words.name: encoding
words.description: memory process

words.name: recall
words.description: retrieval of events or information from the past

words.name: recognition
words.description: subcategory of declarative memory, referring to the ability to recognize previously encountered events, objects, or people

words.name: forgetting
words.description: loss or failure to recall information stored in human memory

words.name: heuristic
words.description: simple strategies, rules or mental processes involved in making quick judgments or decisions

words.name: behavior
words.description: range of actions by entities, living or not, within a system

words.name: learning
words.description: process of acquiring new knowledge

words.name: reinforcement
words.description: consequence that will strengthen an organism's future behavior whenever that behavior is preceded by a specific antecedent stimulus

words.name: punishment
words.description: facet of operant conditioning; change in a human or animal's surroundings that occurs after a given behavior or response which reduces the likelihood of that behavior occurring again in the future

words.name: habituation
words.description: decrease in a behavioral response to a repeated stimulus

words.name: extinction
words.description: fading of non-reinforced conditioned response over time

words.name: stimulus
words.description: in psychology, an object or event that elicits a sensory or behavioral response in an organism

words.name: temperament
words.description: term used to indicate the mixture of the innate aspects of the personality

words.name: attitude
words.description: psychological construct, a mental and emotional entity that inheres in, or characterizes a person

words.name: obedience
words.description: yielding to instructions from an authority figure

words.name: persuasion
words.description: mode of social communication that aims to influence someone else's beliefs, attitudes, or intentions

words.name: attribution
words.description: process by which individuals explain the causes of behavior and events

words.name: empathy
words.description: capacity to understand or feel what another person is experiencing from within the latter's frame of reference

words.name: personality
words.description: psychological characteristics of an individual (usually referring to only humans)

words.name: extraversion
words.description: personality trait

words.name: neuroticism
words.description: personality trait of the tendency to experience negative emotions

words.name: self-concept
words.description: one's internal beliefs about oneself

words.name: self-esteem
words.description: term used in psychology to reflect a person's overall emotional evaluation of their own worth

words.name: phobia
words.description: anxiety disorder defined by a persistent and excessive fear of a concept, an object or a situation

words.name: schizophrenia
words.description: psychotic disorder characterized by emotional responsiveness and disintegration of thought process

words.name: psychosis
words.description: abnormal condition of the mind that involves hallucinations and delusions and is a symptom of any of various disorders or other causes

words.name: anxiety
words.description: unpleasant complex combination of emotions that includes fear, apprehension and worry, and is often accompanied by physical sensations such as palpitations, nausea, chest pain and/or shortness of breath

words.name: depression
words.description: state of low mood and aversion to activity

words.name: psychotherapy
words.description: clinically applied psychology for desired behavior change

words.name: counseling
words.description: branch of psychology

words.name: psychoanalysis
words.description: psychological theory that was founded in 1890 by the Viennese neurologist Sigmund Freud

words.name: id
words.description: psychologist concepts by Sigmund Freud

words.name: synapse
words.description: junction between a nerve fiber of one neuron and another neuron, muscle fiber or glial cell

words.name: neurotransmitter
words.description: endogenous chemicals that transmit signals across a synapse from one neuron to another

words.name: dopamine
words.description: organic chemical that functions both as a hormone and a neurotransmitter

words.name: serotonin
words.description: monoamine neurotransmitter

words.name: reflex
words.description: automatic, subconscious response to a stimulus

words.name: amnesia
words.description: cognitive disorder where the memory is disturbed or lost and involves the loss of memories previously established, loss of the ability to create new memories, continuous high blood pressure and severe shock may also cause amnesia

words.name: sensation
words.description: physiological capacity of organisms that provides data for perception

words.name: reliability
words.description: consistency of a measure in psychometrics and statistics, that is if it produces similar results under consistent conditions

words.name: validity
words.description: extent to which a concept, conclusion or measurement is well-founded and corresponds accurately to the real world

words.name: stress
words.description: psychological feeling of strain and pressure, that could increase the risk of strokes, heart attacks, ulcers, and mental illnesses such as depression and aggravation of a pre-existing condition

words.name: coping
words.description: strategies used during the confrontation with some events to reduce unpleasant emotions

words.name: placebo
words.description: substance or treatment of no therapeutic value

words.name: self-actualization
words.description: term used in various psychology theories

words.name: emotion
words.description: biological states associated with the nervous system

words.name: motivation
words.description: inner state causing goal-directed behavior

words.name: drive
words.description: theory that attempts to define, analyze, or classify psychological drives

words.name: instinct
words.description: inherent inclination of a living organism

words.name: need
words.description: thing that is necessary for an organism to live a healthy life

words.name: mood
words.description: relatively long lasting emotional, internal and subjective state

words.name: affect
words.description: experience of feeling or emotion

words.name: fear
words.description: emotion induced by perceived danger or threat

triples.name: clinical psychology
triples.description: integration of science and clinical knowledge for the purpose of relieving psychologically based dysfunction. see Wikipedia: Clinical psychology (Q199906)

triples.name: cognitive psychology
triples.description: subdiscipline of psychology. see Wikipedia: Cognitive psychology (Q23373)

triples.name: developmental psychology
triples.description: scientific study of changes that occur in human beings over the course of their lives. see Wikipedia: Developmental psychology (Q175002)

triples.name: educational psychology
triples.description: branch of psychology concerned with the scientific study of human learning. see Wikipedia: Educational psychology (Q59157)

triples.name: industrial and organizational psychology
triples.description: branch of psychology. see Wikipedia: Industrial and organizational psychology (Q2045692)

triples.name: personality psychology
triples.description: branch of psychology focused on personality. see Wikipedia: Personality psychology (Q271716)

triples.name: abnormal psychology
triples.description: branch of psychology that studies unusual patterns of behavior, emotion, and thought, which could possibly be understood as a mental disorder. see Wikipedia: Abnormal psychology (Q1987782)

triples.name: health psychology
triples.description: the study of psychological and behavioral processes in health, illness, and healthcare. see Wikipedia: Health psychology (Q1403186)

triples.name: positive psychology
triples.description: scientific study of the positive aspects of the human experience that make life worth living. see Wikipedia: Positive psychology (Q718809)

triples.name: evolutionary psychology
triples.description: branch of psychology. see Wikipedia: Evolutionary psychology (Q219695)

triples.name: forensic psychology
triples.description: the intersection between psychology and the justice system. see Wikipedia: Forensic psychology (Q932219)

triples.name: short-term memory
triples.description: the memory process that deals with the storage, retrieval and modification of information received a short time (up to about 30 minutes) ago. see Wikipedia: Short-term memory (Q18599)

triples.name: long-term memory
triples.description: memory process that deals with the storage, retrieval and modification of information a long time (typically weeks, months or years) after receiving that information. see Wikipedia: Long-term memory (Q18601)

triples.name: working memory
triples.description: cognitive system with a limited capacity that is responsible for temporarily holding information available for processing. see Wikipedia: Working memory (Q11337567)

triples.name: sensory memory
triples.description: bodily experienced sensations from the past. see Wikipedia: Sensory memory (Q1080996)

triples.name: cognitive bias
triples.description: systematic pattern of deviation from norm or rationality in judgment due to subjective perception of reality. see Wikipedia: Cognitive bias (Q1127759)

triples.name: confirmation bias
triples.description: tendency of people to favor information that confirms their beliefs or hypotheses. see Wikipedia: Confirmation bias (Q431498)

triples.name: gestalt psychology
triples.description: theory of mind examining human perception, structures and organizing principles in sensory impressions. see Wikipedia: Gestalt psychology (Q272021)

triples.name: depth perception
triples.description: visual ability to perceive the world in three dimensions (3D). see Wikipedia: Depth perception (Q1749840)

triples.name: optical illusion
triples.description: distortion of the senses or perception of reality, which may reveal how the human brain normally organizes and interprets sensory stimulation. see Wikipedia: Illusion (Q182593)

triples.name: classical conditioning
triples.description: learning procedure in which biologically potent stimulus is paired with a neutral stimulus. see Wikipedia: Classical conditioning (Q212737)

triples.name: operant conditioning
triples.description: learning to anticipate future events on the basis of past experience with the consequences of one's own behavior. see Wikipedia: Operant conditioning (Q847079)

triples.name: attachment theory
triples.description: psychological ethological theory about human relationships. see Wikipedia: Attachment theory (Q909609)

triples.name: cognitive development
triples.description: field of study in neuroscience and psychology. see Wikipedia: Cognitive development (Q2910672)

triples.name: object permanence
triples.description: understanding that objects continue to exist even when they cannot be observed. see Wikipedia: Object permanence (Q1417978)

triples.name: nature versus nurture
triples.description: relative importance of an individual's innate qualities ("nature" in the sense of nativism or innatism) as compared to an individual's personal experiences ("nurture" in the sense of empiricism or behaviorism). see Wikipedia: Nature versus nurture (Q1773859)

triples.name: cognitive dissonance
triples.description: mental stress or discomfort experienced by an individual who holds two or more contradictory beliefs, ideas, or values at the same time. see Wikipedia: Cognitive dissonance (Q209722)

triples.name: bystander effect
triples.description: social psychological phenomenon in which individuals are less likely to offer help to a victim when other people are present. see Wikipedia: Bystander effect (Q230749)

triples.name: social influence
triples.description: alteration of attitudes and behaviors based on outside influences. see Wikipedia: Social influence (Q1307337)

triples.name: trait theory
triples.description: approach to the study of human personality. see Wikipedia: Trait theory (Q18773)

triples.name: Big Five personality traits
triples.description: personality model consisting of five broad dimensions of personality. see Wikipedia: Big Five personality traits (Q378132)

triples.name: mental disorder
triples.description: distressing thought or behavior pattern. see Wikipedia: Mental disorder (Q12135)

triples.name: bipolar disorder
triples.description: mental illness characterized by mood changes. see Wikipedia: Bipolar disorder (Q131755)

triples.name: obsessive-compulsive disorder
triples.description: mental disorder involving obsessions and compulsions. see Wikipedia: Obsessive–compulsive disorder (Q178190)

triples.name: post-traumatic stress disorder
triples.description: psychiatric disorder that developed after experiencing or witnessing a terrifying or life-threatening event. see Wikipedia: Post-traumatic stress disorder (Q202387)

triples.name: cognitive behavioral therapy
triples.description: psychotherapeutic approach that addresses dysfunctional emotions, behaviors and cognitions. see Wikipedia: Cognitive behavioral therapy (Q1147152)

triples.name: defence mechanism
triples.description: unconscious psychological mechanism that reduces anxiety arising from unacceptable or potentially harmful stimuli. see Wikipedia: Defence mechanism (Q2866472)

triples.name: unconscious mind
triples.description: group of psychic characters and processes that are reflected in behaviour, even though not shown in the conciousness. see Wikipedia: Unconscious mind (Q192105)

triples.name: absolute threshold
triples.description: term in neuroscience and psychophysics. see Wikipedia: Absolute threshold (Q2609467)

triples.name: just-noticeable difference
triples.description: amount that a stimulus must be changed to be detected. see Wikipedia: Just-noticeable difference (Q1224386)

triples.name: Weber-Fechner law
triples.description: psychophysics law that the minimum perceptible increase of stimulus is proportional to the stimulus and that intensity of our sensation increases logarithmically. see Wikipedia: Weber–Fechner law (Q835216)

triples.name: signal detection theory
triples.description: analyzed the detection of difficult to detect signals and provides a measure of the quality of the detection. see Wikipedia: Detection theory (Q120811)

triples.name: intelligence quotient
triples.description: score from a test designed to assess intelligence. see Wikipedia: Intelligence quotient (Q170277)

triples.name: Maslow's hierarchy of needs
triples.description: theory in developmental psychology proposed by Abraham Maslow, comprising a five-tier model of human needs: physiological, safety, love and belonging, esteem, and self-actualization. see Wikipedia: Maslow's hierarchy of needs (Q179515)

triples.name: Dunbar's number
triples.description: value important in sociology and anthropology. see Wikipedia: Dunbar's number (Q1137451)

triples.name: the magical number seven
triples.description: 1956 thesis by George Miller on working memory capacity. see Wikipedia: The Magical Number Seven, Plus or Minus Two (Q1542532)

triples.name: mental age
triples.description: the age level at which a person performs intellectually, as measured by a standardized test

triples.name: chronological age
triples.description: the actual age of a person measured in years since birth

triples.name: Weber fraction
triples.description: the constant ratio of the just-noticeable difference to the magnitude of the original stimulus

triples.name: stimulus intensity
triples.description: the physical magnitude of a stimulus presented to a sense organ

triples.name: perceived intensity
triples.description: the subjective magnitude of a sensation as experienced by a person

triples.name: scaling constant
triples.description: a constant of proportionality relating a sensation to the physical stimulus that produces it

triples.name: Stevens exponent
triples.description: the exponent in Stevens' power law that depends on the type of stimulus being judged

triples.name: raw score
triples.description: an original measurement or test result before it is converted to a standardized score

triples.name: standard score
triples.description: the number of standard deviations by which a raw score lies above or below the mean

triples.name: standard deviation
triples.description: a measure of the amount of variation or dispersion of a set of values around their mean

sources.name: by definition
sources.description: a value that is fixed by an unambiguous definition or convention rather than measured

sources.name: psychology reference data
sources.description: commonly cited estimates from psychology research used as reference values
